ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general roti / whole wheat flatbread class. No specific butter tandoori roti entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and rolls — whole wheat / wholemeal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tandoori roti code. A00LC (wholemeal bread) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for butter tandoori roti. General flatbread concepts are insufficiently specific; null used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard butter tandoori roti piece weighs approximately 80 g, which is a typical single-serving portion as served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Roti (whole wheat, with butter) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted for butter-finished preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain tandoori roti (whole wheat) at approximately 264 kcal/100g. Butter finishing adds approximately 15–20 kcal/100g based on typical 5–8 g butter per 80 g roti. Values adapted to reflect butter-finished product. Core macros cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entry for whole wheat flatbread (FDC ID 172892) to validate protein and carbohydrate fractions.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of whole wheat chapati / roti — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for butter tandoori roti specifically. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of 62 for whole wheat chapati/roti (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Tandoori roti is made from the same whole wheat atta dough and baked at high heat; the butter finishing adds fat which may marginally lower GI but the effect is small. GI of 62 (medium) is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. GL per serving = (62 × 40.2 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 24.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100g; ~248 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit butter tandoori roti to one piece per meal, as the combined sodium from the dough and salted butter contributes meaningfully to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; choosing unsalted butter reduces sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and moderate glycemic load (~24.9 per 80 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume butter tandoori roti in controlled portions (one piece per meal) given its medium GI of 62 and glycemic load of approximately 25; pairing with high-fibre vegetables or protein-rich dal can blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~2.9 g/100g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, butter tandoori roti should be consumed in moderation; the saturated fat contributed by butter (~2.3 g per 80 g serving) adds to daily saturated fat intake, and substituting with a small amount of unsaturated oil or requesting less butter can improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Tandoori Roti is a whole wheat flatbread (roti) made from atta (whole wheat flour), water, and salt, shaped into a round disc and baked directly on the inner wall of a tandoor — a cylindrical clay oven fired to temperatures exceeding 400°C. The intense radiant heat produces a bread with a slightly charred, blistered exterior and a soft, chewy interior. Immediately after baking, the roti is brushed or smeared with butter, which melts into the surface and imparts a rich, slightly savoury flavour and a glossy sheen. Compared to plain tandoori roti, the butter version is more calorie-dense (approximately 284 kcal/100g) and contains a modest amount of saturated fat (~2.9 g/100g). The whole wheat base provides dietary fibre (~3.8 g/100g), B vitamins, iron, and magnesium. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. In Singapore, butter tandoori roti is served in Indian restaurants — particularly North Indian and Punjabi establishments — as an accompaniment to curries, dal, or tandoori grilled meats. It is vegetarian and halal-certified in most restaurant contexts. Clinically, the moderate sodium (~310 mg/100g) and saturated fat content warrant attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter tandoori roti?
What is the GI of tandoori roti?
Is tandoori roti good for diabetics?
Difference between butter naan and butter tandoori roti

confidence_reason: No exact entry for butter tandoori roti exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain tandoori roti data with a calculated butter contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati/roti. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted rather than directly measured for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain tandoori roti baseline (~264 kcal/100g) with an estimated butter contribution of approximately 5–8 g butter per 80 g roti, yielding ~284 kcal/100g for the butter-finished product. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as whole wheat flatbread contains negligible vitamin C and the value is consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for this food category — confirmed zero per approved sources. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food category and is not reliably present in plain flatbreads; the butter contribution is too small to provide a meaningful reportable value. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for tandoori roti and is not available from the adapted source. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on whole wheat chapati/roti from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). 6. GL per serving calculated as (62 × 40.16 g available carbohydrate in 80 g serving) / 100 ≈ 24.9. 7. Serving size of 80 g is a standard single-piece estimate for restaurant-served tandoori roti in Singapore. 8. Halal status is context-dependent; most Indian Muslim restaurants in Singapore serve halal-certified versions, but this cannot be universally guaranteed.
